note_data.title: Integration
keyPoints: **Indefinite integration** is the reverse process of differentiation, written as ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + c where F'(x) = f(x)
**Constant of integration** (+c) must always be included in indefinite integrals to represent the family of possible antiderivatives
**Definite integration** ∫[a to b]f(x)dx gives a numerical value representing the area between the curve and x-axis from x = a to x = b
**Fundamental Theorem of Calculus** states that ∫[a to b]f(x)dx = [F(x)][a to b] = F(b) - F(a)
**Area under a curve** is calculated using definite integration, taking absolute values when the function is negative
**Area between two curves** f(x) and g(x) is found using ∫[a to b]|f(x) - g(x)|dx where curves intersect at x = a and x = b
**Volume of revolution** about x-axis is V = π∫[a to b][f(x)]²dx when curve y = f(x) is rotated
**Volume of revolution** about y-axis is V = π∫[c to d][g(y)]²dy when curve x = g(y) is rotated
**Integration by substitution** requires identifying u, finding du/dx, changing limits if definite, and substituting back
**Integration by parts** uses ∫udv = uv - ∫vdu, choosing u as the function that becomes simpler when differentiated

definitions.definition: The general antiderivative of a function f(x), written as ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + c, where F'(x) = f(x) and c is the constant of integration
The numerical value ∫[a to b]f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) representing the net area between the curve y = f(x) and the x-axis from x = a to x = b
The volume of the solid formed when a curve is rotated completely about an axis, calculated using π∫[a to b][f(x)]²dx for rotation about the x-axis
A technique for integrating products of functions using the formula ∫udv = uv - ∫vdu

howToAnswer.instruction: Calculate the integral showing all working steps including the constant of integration for indefinite integrals
Demonstrate the result by working through the integration process step by step until you reach the given answer
Find the numerical value for definite integrals or volumes, showing substitution of limits clearly

modelAnswer.question: Find the area of the region bounded by the curve y = x² - 4x + 3, the x-axis, and the lines x = 1 and x = 4.
modelAnswer.answer: First find where curve crosses x-axis: x² - 4x + 3 = 0, so (x-1)(x-3) = 0, giving x = 1, 3. Curve is below x-axis between x = 1 and x = 3, above between x = 3 and x = 4. Area = |∫[1 to 3](x² - 4x + 3)dx| + ∫[3 to 4](x² - 4x + 3)dx = |[x³/3 - 2x² + 3x][1 to 3]| + [x³/3 - 2x² + 3x][3 to 4] = |0 - (1/3 - 2 + 3)| + (64/3 - 32 + 12) - 0 = 4/3 + 4/3 = 8/3
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Recognizing need for two separate integrals due to sign change
Mark 3: Correct integration and substitution of limits

commonMistakes: Never forget the constant of integration (+c) in indefinite integrals — always include it even if not explicitly asked
Students often ignore when the curve goes below the x-axis in area calculations — must use absolute values or split the integral
Never substitute limits before integrating when using substitution method — change the limits or substitute back to original variable first
Students frequently confuse volume formulas — remember π∫[f(x)]²dx for rotation about x-axis, not just π∫f(x)dx
Never use integration by parts when simple power rule or standard results work — choose the simplest method first
examinerTips: Integration appears in every Paper 1 and Paper 2 — master the basic techniques thoroughly
Always sketch the region for area and volume questions to understand the problem geometry
Show clear substitution of limits in definite integrals for method marks
Volume of revolution questions often worth 6-8 marks — check your π factor and squared terms carefully
formulas: Volume about x-axis: V = π∫[a to b][f(x)]²dx
Volume about y-axis: V = π∫[c to d][g(y)]²dy
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Sketch showing a curve y = f(x) with shaded region between the curve and x-axis from x = a to x = b, with clear labels for the function, axes, and limits of integration
